title: Your Own MCP Server: The First Tool
subtitle: The Python or TypeScript SDK, a server with one tool, and the mcp-builder skill to help

theory.md:
Ready-made servers cover popular services, but your work reality is unique: the company's internal API, a homegrown customer database, a local script only you know how to run. How do you give Claude access to all of it? Write **your own MCP server**.

It sounds more serious than it is: a minimal server with one tool is a couple dozen lines. The protocol has official SDKs that take on all the "plumbing" — the connection, the message format, the spec-compliant exchange:

- **Python SDK** — github.com/modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk;
- **TypeScript SDK** — github.com/modelcontextprotocol/typescript-sdk.

All that's left for you is to describe **what exactly your server can do**. Which language to pick? The one already installed on your machine and known to you (or your team): the protocol and the logic are the same, only the syntax differs. The examples ahead are in Python — in TypeScript it's all the same: you create a server, register tools with descriptions and parameter schemas, and launch the transport.

The skeleton of any simple server is three steps:

1. **Create the server** and give it a name — the host will see it under that name.
2. **Register a tool**: an ordinary function with typed parameters and a description. The SDK builds a JSON schema from it that the model will see.
3. **Launch the transport** — for a local server that's stdio: the client will launch your program and talk to it over standard input/output.

Here's how it looks in the Python SDK (the high-level FastMCP interface; check the exact API details against the SDK docs — they evolve):

theory.title: server.py — a server with one tool

theory.md:
What happens next? When the host connects to the server, the MCP client asks: "what tools do you have?" The SDK answers for you: the name `define_term`, the description from the docstring, the parameter `term` of type string — all of this the model will see and use to decide when to call your tool.

An important subtlety of stdio servers: **standard output (stdout) is occupied by the protocol** — messages between the client and the server travel through it. If your code prints something of its own there — say, a debug `print("started!")` — those bytes wedge into the message exchange, the client gets garbage, and the connection breaks. For debugging, write to stderr or a log file: the protocol doesn't touch them.

theory.title: Connecting your server to Claude Code
theory.code:
# Add it: after -- comes the launch command for our file
claude mcp add team-glossary -- python server.py

# Start a session and check
claude
> /mcp   # the team-glossary server should be connected
> What is SLA?   # Claude will call define_term itself

theory.md:
You don't have to write the server by hand from scratch. Anthropic's official skills library (github.com/anthropics/skills) has **mcp-builder** — a skill that teaches Claude to build high-quality MCP servers: how to design tools, how to write descriptions, how to lay out code in Python (FastMCP) or TypeScript.

The workflow looks like this: you connect the skill, describe the task to Claude Code — "make an MCP server for our orders API: get an order by number, list of orders for a day" — and the agent with the skill generates a server following best practices. Your work shifts from "writing code" to "describing precisely which tools are needed and what they should return."

theory.explanation: The model picks a tool by its name and description — it has nothing else to go on. "process / processes data" says nothing about what the function does, when to call it, or what data is. The good version answers all three questions: a concrete action-name, the parameter format with an example, and a clear hint about the situation in which the tool is needed.

theory.md: The official Python SDK's README describes it as a full implementation of the MCP specification: servers can provide tools, resources, and prompts, and the high-level FastMCP interface lets you declare tools with decorators on top of ordinary functions — parameter schemas are inferred from type annotations. The TypeScript SDK is built the same way.

Your server is code that will run on every tool call. Don't hard-code secrets into it (read API keys from environment variables), and don't give tools more permissions than the task needs: a "read an order" tool shouldn't be able to delete the database.

Debug the server in short cycles: change the code → restart the Claude Code session → `/mcp` → ask a question that should call the tool. If the model doesn't call your tool — it's almost always the description: it simply didn't understand that the tool fits the task.

tasks.instruction: Assemble, in the right order, the path from an idea to a working MCP server with one tool.
blocks: Create the server through the SDK and give it a name
Write a tool function with typed parameters and a clear description
Connect the server: claude mcp add my-server -- python server.py
Check in a session via /mcp and a test question that triggers the tool
distractors: Retrain the Claude model on your API's data
Publish the server in a registry before it has run even once
tasks.explanation: The order repeats the three steps from the lesson plus connecting and checking: server → tool → transport → claude mcp add → test via /mcp. Retraining the model isn't needed at all — the server only describes tools — and publishing before the first run breaks the core principle: test first, then share.

tasks.instruction: The server is written, but the connection to Claude Code keeps dropping with a protocol error. Find the line that breaks the stdio server.
lines: """Returns the definition of a term from the team glossary."""
tasks.explanation: On a stdio server, standard output (stdout) is entirely occupied by MCP protocol messages. The print(...) line wedges extraneous text into that stream — the client gets garbage instead of a valid message, and the connection drops. Write debug messages to stderr or a log file, but not to stdout.

tasks.question: How does the model learn which tools your server has and when to call them?
options: It reads the entire source of server.py
The MCP client asks the server for a list of tools: names, descriptions, and parameter schemas
The tools have to be listed in the system prompt by hand
The model figures them out by trial and error
tasks.explanation: On connecting, the client asks the server about its capabilities, and the server returns tool declarations: name, description, JSON schema of parameters (the SDK builds it from type annotations). Those descriptions are the only thing the model sees, so whether a tool gets called appropriately depends on their quality.
tasks.hint: Recall what happens the moment a server connects — who asks whom, and what.

tasks.instruction: Your server is ready in the file server.py. Connect it to Claude Code under the name team-glossary (`claude mcp add team-glossary -- python server.py`), start a session (`claude`), and check the connection (/mcp).

steps.output: Added stdio MCP server team-glossary with command: python server.py to local config
steps.failHint: The formula: claude mcp add team-glossary -- python server.py (after -- comes the launch command for your file).
A session is started with the claude command

steps.output:
MCP Servers:

  ✔ team-glossary — connected
    Tools: define_term (Returns the definition of a term from the team glossary)

  Total: 1 server, 1 tool.
steps.failHint: The status of servers inside a session is shown by the /mcp slash command

tasks.successMessage: The server is connected and the tool is visible — notice that /mcp shows exactly the description you wrote in the docstring. The model will rely on it when deciding when to call define_term.

tasks.instruction:
Mission: your first own MCP server. **Claude Code and either Python or Node.js must be installed.**

1. Ask Claude Code: "Create a minimal MCP server in Python (FastMCP from the official SDK) with one tool of my choice" — for example, a glossary of your work terms or a unit converter. If your skills library has mcp-builder — connect it.
2. Study the generated code: find all three steps — creating the server, registering the tool, launching the transport.
3. Connect the server: `claude mcp add my-first -- python server.py` (or the corresponding command for Node).
4. Restart the session, check `/mcp`, and ask a question the server should answer.
5. Improve the tool's description and make sure the model starts calling it more confidently.
checklist: The server is created and contains exactly one tool
I found all three steps in the code: server, tool, transport
/mcp shows the server in the connected state
Claude called my tool and returned data from it
There are no secrets and no stray prints to stdout in the code
